card.indications: Management of severe pain requiring an opioid when alternatives are inadequate; selected products are used for cough suppression.
card.mechanism: Activates mu-opioid receptors in the central nervous system, reducing pain perception and suppressing cough while depressing respiration and gastrointestinal motility.
card.dosing: Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration. Immediate-release dosing is product specific and often every 4 to 6 hours as needed. Extended-release products are reserved for opioid-tolerant or chronic-pain patients according to labeling.
card.routeAdministration: Assess pain, sedation, respiratory rate, blood pressure, and bowel function before dosing. Swallow extended-release products whole and do not crush. Track total acetaminophen when using combination products.
card.contraindications: Significant respiratory depression, acute or severe bronchial asthma without monitoring, known or suspected gastrointestinal obstruction, and serious hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings include addiction, abuse, misuse, life-threatening respiratory depression, accidental ingestion, neonatal opioid withdrawal, and profound sedation or death with benzodiazepines and other CNS depressants.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Constipation, nausea, vomiting, drowsiness, dizziness, itching, and dry mouth.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Fatal respiratory depression, apnea, severe hypotension, ileus, adrenal insufficiency, serotonin syndrome, anaphylaxis, overdose, and dependence.
card.interactions: Benzodiazepines, alcohol, sleep drugs, antihistamines, gabapentinoids, and other CNS depressants increase respiratory risk. CYP3A inhibitors can increase exposure; inducers can reduce effect or precipitate withdrawal.
card.monitoring: Monitor pain relief, respiratory status, sedation, blood pressure, bowel function, misuse risk, and total combination-product ingredients. Consider naloxone access.
card.patientTeaching: Take exactly as prescribed, avoid alcohol and sedatives, do not drive, store locked, and seek emergency help for slow breathing or inability to awaken.
card.nutrition: Take with food if nauseated. Increase fluids and fiber unless restricted. Avoid alcohol and check OTC products for acetaminophen.

card.indications: Management of severe pain requiring an opioid when alternative treatments are inadequate.
card.mechanism: Potent mu-opioid receptor agonist that alters pain perception and response and depresses respiratory and gastrointestinal function.
card.dosing: Dose is highly individualized by route, opioid tolerance, organ function, and pain severity. It is much more potent than morphine; conversion errors can be fatal. Extended-release products are for appropriately opioid-tolerant patients.
card.routeAdministration: Verify formulation, concentration, and route carefully. Give IV doses slowly and monitor closely. Do not crush extended-release tablets. Assess pain, sedation, and respiratory rate before and after administration.
card.contraindications: Significant respiratory depression, acute or severe bronchial asthma without monitoring, gastrointestinal obstruction, and serious hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Boxed opioid warnings include addiction, abuse, misuse, life-threatening respiratory depression, accidental ingestion, neonatal withdrawal, and dangerous sedation with CNS depressants. Medication errors between concentrations are a major risk.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Sedation, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, constipation, itching, sweating, and dry mouth.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Fatal respiratory depression, apnea, severe hypotension, ileus, adrenal insufficiency, serotonin syndrome, anaphylaxis, and overdose.
card.interactions: Benzodiazepines, alcohol, anesthetics, sleep drugs, antihistamines, and other CNS depressants increase respiratory depression. MAO inhibitors and serotonergic drugs may cause serious reactions.
card.monitoring: Monitor pain, respiratory rate, oxygenation, sedation score, blood pressure, bowel function, renal and hepatic status, and misuse risk.
card.patientTeaching: Do not mix with alcohol or sedatives, do not drive, use only the prescribed formulation, and keep naloxone available when recommended.
card.nutrition: Take oral doses with food if nausea occurs. Maintain fluids and fiber unless contraindicated.

card.indications: Relief of itching due to allergic conditions, short-term management of anxiety, and preoperative or procedural sedation depending on formulation.
card.mechanism: Blocks peripheral and central H1 receptors and has anticholinergic and sedative effects.
card.dosing: Dose varies by indication and age. Use lower doses in older adults and hepatic or renal impairment. IM use is formulation specific; avoid IV or subcutaneous administration.
card.routeAdministration: May be taken with or without food. Measure liquids accurately. Deep IM administration only when ordered. Assess sedation, anticholinergic burden, and fall risk.
card.contraindications: Early pregnancy under some product labels, prolonged QT interval, and serious hypersensitivity to hydroxyzine, cetirizine, or related compounds.
card.warnings: Can cause marked sedation, respiratory depression with other CNS depressants, anticholinergic toxicity, QT prolongation, torsades de pointes, and falls.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Drowsiness, dry mouth, dizziness, blurred vision, constipation, and urinary retention.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Torsades de pointes, severe respiratory depression, anaphylaxis, seizures, confusion, and severe anticholinergic toxicity.
card.interactions: Alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, sleep medications, and other sedatives increase CNS depression. Other QT-prolonging drugs increase arrhythmia risk.
card.monitoring: Monitor symptom relief, sedation, respiratory status, ECG risk factors, urinary retention, constipation, confusion, and falls.
card.patientTeaching: Avoid alcohol and driving. Report fainting, palpitations, difficulty urinating, severe confusion, or trouble breathing.
card.nutrition: May be taken with food if stomach upset occurs. Maintain hydration and fiber for dry mouth and constipation.

card.indications: Relief of mild to moderate pain, fever, dysmenorrhea, osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and selected acute inflammatory conditions.
card.mechanism: Reversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase-1 and cyclooxygenase-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis.
card.dosing: OTC and prescription dosing vary by indication, age, and renal or GI risk. Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration and avoid duplicate NSAID therapy.
card.routeAdministration: Give with food or milk if GI upset occurs. Ensure hydration. IV products require product-specific dilution and infusion. Avoid perioperative use in coronary artery bypass graft surgery.
card.contraindications: History of aspirin- or NSAID-induced asthma or allergic reaction, perioperative CABG pain, active serious GI bleeding, and severe hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings for serious cardiovascular thrombotic events and GI bleeding, ulceration, or perforation. Additional risks include renal injury, hypertension, heart failure, hepatotoxicity, severe skin reactions, and fetal harm later in pregnancy.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dyspepsia, nausea, heartburn, abdominal pain, dizziness, headache, edema, and increased blood pressure.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: GI hemorrhage or perforation, myocardial infarction, stroke, acute kidney injury, heart failure, anaphylaxis, severe skin reactions, and liver injury.
card.interactions: Anticoagulants, antiplatelets, SSRIs, corticosteroids, and other NSAIDs increase bleeding. ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and diuretics increase renal risk. Ibuprofen may interfere with aspirin's antiplatelet effect depending on timing.
card.monitoring: Monitor pain and fever response, blood pressure, edema, renal function, CBC, GI bleeding, liver tests when indicated, and concurrent NSAID use.
card.patientTeaching: Take only the recommended amount, avoid combining with other NSAIDs, and report black stools, vomiting blood, chest pain, shortness of breath, or reduced urine.
card.nutrition: Take with food or milk if needed and maintain hydration. Avoid excessive alcohol because it increases GI bleeding risk.

card.indications: Treatment of depression; selected formulations are also used for childhood enuresis after other causes are excluded.
card.mechanism: Inhibits reuptake of norepinephrine and serotonin and has anticholinergic, antihistamine, and alpha-adrenergic blocking effects.
card.dosing: Start low and titrate gradually. Older adults and patients with cardiac disease require cautious dosing. Enuresis dosing is age and weight specific and usually given in the evening.
card.routeAdministration: May be taken with food. Bedtime dosing can reduce daytime sedation. Do not stop abruptly. Secure the medication because overdose can be rapidly fatal.
card.contraindications: Concurrent or recent MAO inhibitor use, acute recovery after myocardial infarction, and serious hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Boxed warning for suicidal thoughts and behaviors in younger patients. Risks include fatal arrhythmias in overdose, orthostatic hypotension, seizures, anticholinergic toxicity, mania, serotonin syndrome, and angle-closure glaucoma.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision, urinary retention, sedation, dizziness, sweating, weight gain, and orthostatic hypotension.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Ventricular arrhythmias, heart block, seizures, severe hypotension, suicidal behavior, serotonin syndrome, paralytic ileus, and agranulocytosis.
card.interactions: MAO inhibitors are contraindicated. Other serotonergic drugs increase serotonin syndrome. Alcohol and sedatives increase CNS depression. Anticholinergic drugs increase toxicity. CYP2D6 inhibitors can raise levels.
card.monitoring: Monitor mood and suicide risk, ECG in at-risk patients, blood pressure, anticholinergic effects, falls, seizures, and adherence.
card.patientTeaching: Take exactly as prescribed, avoid alcohol, rise slowly, and report suicidal thoughts, fainting, palpitations, urinary retention, severe constipation, or eye pain.
card.nutrition: May be taken with food. Maintain hydration and fiber to reduce constipation.
